evals.name: investigation-board
evals.prompt: I'm debugging a memory leak in our Node.js API. The /api/users endpoint is leaking memory on every request. I found a suspicious closure in src/handlers/users.ts that captures the entire request object, and the heap snapshot shows growing EventEmitter listeners. Can you set up an investigation board on the canvas so I can see the full picture?
evals.expected_output: Creates multiple nodes (bug description, code file, heap findings, hypothesis) connected with edges, arranged in a tree layout. Uses appropriate node types (markdown for findings, file for source, status for investigation progress).

assertions.name: creates-multiple-nodes
assertions.description: Creates at least 3 nodes representing different aspects of the investigation

assertions.name: uses-edges
assertions.description: Connects nodes with labeled edges showing relationships between evidence

assertions.name: uses-tree-or-arrange
assertions.description: Arranges the nodes using tree layout or explicit positioning for readability

assertions.name: uses-appropriate-types
assertions.description: Uses file nodes for source code and markdown/status for findings rather than all markdown

evals.name: architecture-diagram
evals.prompt: We're building a microservices backend with these services: API Gateway (Express, port 3000), Auth Service (JWT + OAuth2, port 3001), User Service (CRUD, port 3002), PostgreSQL (port 5432), and Redis (caching, port 6379). The API Gateway routes to Auth and User services, both Auth and User read/write to PostgreSQL, and User Service uses Redis for caching. Can you visualize this architecture on the canvas?
evals.expected_output: Creates nodes for each service with relevant details, connects them with flow/depends-on edges with descriptive labels, groups related services, and arranges in a readable layout.

assertions.name: all-services-present
assertions.description: Creates nodes for all 5 services/datastores mentioned

assertions.name: correct-connections
assertions.description: Creates edges matching the described relationships (Gateway->Auth, Gateway->User, Auth->PG, User->PG, User->Redis)

assertions.name: edge-labels
assertions.description: Uses descriptive edge labels (not bare unlabeled edges)

assertions.name: layout-applied
assertions.description: Applies auto-arrange or uses explicit positioning for readable layout

evals.name: task-plan-tracking
evals.prompt: I need to implement a new payment feature. Here's the plan: 1) Define payment schema (done), 2) Build Stripe integration (in progress), 3) Create payment API routes (blocked by #2), 4) Add webhook handlers (blocked by #3), 5) Write integration tests (blocked by #4), 6) Update API docs (can start anytime). Show this on the canvas as a task board with dependencies.
evals.expected_output: Creates status nodes for each task with appropriate colors (green=done, yellow=in-progress, red=blocked, blue=ready), connects with depends-on edges, and arranges to show dependency flow.

assertions.name: six-task-nodes
assertions.description: Creates exactly 6 task nodes, one for each task

assertions.name: semantic-colors
assertions.description: Uses green for done, yellow for in-progress, red for blocked tasks

assertions.name: dependency-edges
assertions.description: Creates depends-on edges matching the described blocking relationships

assertions.name: status-node-type
assertions.description: Uses status node type (not markdown) for task items

evals.name: read-pinned-context
evals.prompt: I've pinned some nodes on the canvas that I want you to look at. Can you read what I've pinned and summarize what you think I'm focusing on?
evals.expected_output: Reads canvas://pinned-context resource, summarizes the pinned nodes' content, identifies spatial neighborhoods, and explains what the human appears to be focusing on based on the pinned context and nearby nodes.

assertions.name: reads-pinned-context
assertions.description: Reads the canvas://pinned-context MCP resource (not just canvas_get_layout)

assertions.name: summarizes-content
assertions.description: Provides a meaningful summary of what the pinned nodes contain

assertions.name: interprets-intent
assertions.description: Makes an interpretation of what the human is focusing on based on pinned context

evals.name: code-exploration-files
evals.prompt: I'm trying to understand how the authentication flow works in this project. Can you put the relevant auth files on the canvas so I can see how they connect? The main files are src/auth/login.ts, src/auth/middleware.ts, src/auth/jwt.ts, and src/routes/auth.ts.
evals.expected_output: Creates file nodes for each mentioned file (content auto-loads), relies on code graph to auto-detect import dependencies, groups auth files together, and reads canvas://code-graph for dependency analysis.

assertions.name: uses-file-nodes
assertions.description: Creates file type nodes (not markdown with pasted code) for source files

assertions.name: all-files-added
assertions.description: Adds all 4 mentioned files to the canvas

assertions.name: groups-files
assertions.description: Groups the auth files together in a canvas group

evals.name: status-dashboard-updates
evals.prompt: I'm running a deployment pipeline. Create a dashboard on the canvas showing: Build (passing), Unit Tests (running), Integration Tests (queued), Deploy to Staging (queued). Then update it - unit tests just passed and integration tests are now running.
evals.expected_output: Creates status nodes with semantic colors, arranges as grid dashboard, then updates nodes in place using canvas_update_node (not delete+recreate) to reflect new state.

assertions.name: initial-dashboard
assertions.description: Creates 4 status nodes with appropriate initial colors

assertions.name: updates-in-place
assertions.description: Uses canvas_update_node to change status (not remove+add)

assertions.name: grid-layout
assertions.description: Arranges nodes in grid layout for dashboard view

assertions.name: color-transitions
assertions.description: Updates colors to reflect new state (unit tests -> green, integration -> yellow)

evals.name: snapshot-before-changes
evals.prompt: I want to reorganize the canvas to explore a different approach, but I don't want to lose what's there now. Can you save the current state, clear it, and set up a fresh workspace for the new approach? If it doesn't work out I want to go back.
evals.expected_output: Takes a named snapshot of current state, then clears the canvas. Mentions that canvas_restore can bring back the old state. Does NOT just clear without snapshotting.

assertions.name: takes-snapshot
assertions.description: Saves a snapshot with canvas_snapshot before clearing

assertions.name: clears-after-snapshot
assertions.description: Clears the canvas after (not before) saving the snapshot

assertions.name: mentions-restore
assertions.description: Tells the user how to restore (canvas_restore) if they want to go back
